source_paper_title: Can Learning Be Explained by Local Optimality in Robust Low-Rank Matrix Recovery?

title: Prove or disprove saddle-escaping behavior of subgradient method at true solutions
background_latex:
Consider robust low-rank matrix recovery from linear measurements with additive noise. Let \(X^\star\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) be an unknown rank-\(r\) matrix, and let \(k\ge r\) be a search rank. The measurement operator \(\mathcal{A}:\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\to\mathbb{R}^m\) is defined by \(\mathcal{A}(X) = (\langle A_1,X\rangle,\dots,\langle A_m,X\rangle)^\top\), and observations are \(y=\mathcal{A}(X^\star)+\epsilon\in\mathbb{R}^m\). The factorized (Burer--Monteiro) parameterization is \(X=W_1W_2\) with \(W_1\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times k}\), \(W_2\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d_2}\), and the nonsmooth empirical \(\ell_1\) loss is
\[
 f_{\ell_1}(W_1,W_2) := \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \bigl|y_i-\langle A_i, W_1W_2\rangle\bigr|.
\]
A pair \((W_1^\star,W_2^\star)\) is called a true solution if \(W_1^\star W_2^\star=X^\star\). The paper establishes regimes in which true solutions are (Clarke) critical points of \(f_{\ell_1}\) and, moreover, are strict saddle points in the sense that there exist a direction \(v\) and constants \(c>0\), \(\bar\gamma>0\) such that \(f_{\ell_1}(W^\star+\gamma v)-f_{\ell_1}(W^\star)\le -c\gamma^2\) for all \(0\le\gamma\le\bar\gamma\).

A generic first-order method discussed is the (deterministic) subgradient method applied to \(f_{\ell_1}\): given \(W^{(0)}\), iterate \(W^{(t+1)}=W^{(t)}-\eta_t g^{(t)}\), where \(g^{(t)}\in\partial f_{\ell_1}(W^{(t)})\) is a chosen subgradient and \(\eta_t>0\) is a stepsize sequence (e.g., decaying). The paper discusses strict-saddle-escaping guarantees known for certain proximal/perturbed methods on related nonsmooth/weakly convex problems, and introduces the notion of active strict saddles (from prior work) versus non-active strict saddles.

The empirical phenomenon highlighted in the paper is that, in certain regimes where true solutions are strict saddle points of \(f_{\ell_1}\), the subgradient method with small/random initialization nonetheless often converges to a true solution.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish (prove or disprove) that the (unperturbed) subgradient method with random initialization escapes strict saddle points of the nonsmooth \(\ell_1\) loss \(f_{\ell_1}\) in robust low-rank matrix recovery, when the strict saddle point corresponds to a true solution \((W_1^\star,W_2^\star)\) with \(W_1^\star W_2^\star=X^\star\).

Equivalently, characterize whether such true-solution strict saddles are (in the sense of nonsmooth dynamics) almost surely avoided/escaped by the subgradient method, or whether there exist regimes/initializations/stepsize rules for which the subgradient iterates can converge to these strict saddle points with nonzero probability (or systematically).

location_in_paper: Section 8 (Discussion and Future Directions), page 40 (PDF page 40/45, arXiv v3, 4 Apr 2025).
open_problem_quote: Indeed, we view proving or disproving the above conjectures as a compelling challenge for future research.
subject_classification: Nonsmooth nonconvex optimization (optimization landscape and first-order methods)
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can simulate subgradient dynamics on many random matrix-sensing/completion instances and empirically test convergence versus escape from the true-solution strict saddles, but turning this into a formal counterexample depends on modeling choices (stepsize schedule, subgradient selection rule) and does not yield a finite, easily checkable certificate.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Automatically evaluating candidate proofs or general escape criteria is not straightforward. While numerical experiments could be automated, the open question is primarily theoretical (almost-sure escape vs convergence under nonsmooth dynamics), and there is no clear programmatic fitness signal that correlates with mathematical correctness beyond empirical behavior.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
